title: Gingidia trifoliolata
intro: Gingidia trifoliolata, also known as New Zealand angelica or native parsnip, is a perennial plant native to New Zealand and eastern Australia, characterized by its fern-like foliage and small white flowers. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects, which may contribute to the relief of various health conditions such as arthritis and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Gingidia trifoliolata include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for the plant's medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Gingidia trifoliolata include teas, infusions, and extracts, which are often used in traditional medicine and may be available in health food stores or through online retailers.
taxonomy: Gingidia trifoliolata is classified within the family Apiaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the carrot or parsley family. This family is further divided into several subfamilies and tribes, with Gingidia trifoliolata being a member of the tribe Apioideae. The genus Gingidia is native to New Zealand and is closely related to other genera within the Apiaceae family. The species G. trifoliolata is a rare and endemic plant found in the wild, primarily in the northern parts of the South Island. It is a small to medium-sized perennial herb that grows in a variety of habitats.
